content.text: Due Diligence Evidence Repositories and Renewals

A due diligence review is only as valuable as the evidence it produces and how well that evidence is maintained. Organizations that complete thorough vendor assessments but store the results in email attachments, personal drives, or spreadsheets quickly lose the ability to retrieve evidence under audit, track renewal deadlines, or demonstrate ongoing compliance. A structured evidence repository transforms a point-in-time assessment into a continuously maintained compliance asset.

The evidence repository holds every artifact collected during vendor due diligence, together with the metadata needed to manage renewals and retrieve documents under audit pressure. The repository scope spans both the initial assessment and all subsequent annual renewals.
Annually + on every vendor notification of change

Under a SOC 2 audit of your own organization, your auditors will request evidence that you perform vendor due diligence. The repository must include not just the vendor artifacts but also internal records: who reviewed each artifact, what findings were identified, and who approved the risk acceptance decision.

The repository can be implemented in various tools depending on your organization's existing infrastructure. The key requirement is that documents are version-controlled, access-controlled, searchable by vendor and artifact type, and linked to renewal calendar events.

Governance, Risk, and Compliance (GRC) platforms (ServiceNow GRC, OneTrust, Vanta, Drata) provide purpose-built vendor risk management modules with built-in renewal tracking, evidence upload, questionnaire distribution, and reporting. For organizations already using a GRC platform for IT compliance, this is the preferred option because it integrates vendor risk with other control evidence.
automated renewal alerts, built-in questionnaire workflow, audit-ready reports, integration with contract lifecycle management.
per-vendor licensing costs; requires configuration and ongoing maintenance; questionnaire customization may be limited in lower tiers.

For organizations without a GRC platform, a structured SharePoint library or Confluence space with consistent metadata columns (vendor name, artifact type, expiry date, review date, risk tier) provides a workable repository. Renewal alerts can be driven by SharePoint column alerts or Power Automate flows.

## SharePoint Library Metadata Columns

Column: Vendor Name (text, required)
Column: Artifact Type (choice: SOC2 | ISO27001 | PenTest | DPA | SIG | BAA | TIA | SubprocessorList | RiskRegister)
Column: Risk Tier (choice: Tier1 | Tier2 | Tier3)
Column: Artifact Date (date: the date the document was issued or signed)
Column: Expiry Date (date: when this artifact is no longer valid for compliance purposes)
Column: Renewal Alert Sent (yes/no: track whether 90-day alert has been issued)
Column: Reviewed By (person: InfoSec reviewer name)
Column: Review Date (date)
Column: Risk Acceptance (choice: Accepted | Conditionally Accepted | Rejected | Pending)
Column: Accepted By (person: CISO or delegate)
Column: Notes (multi-line text)

## Power Automate Flow: 90-Day Renewal Alert
Trigger: Scheduled (daily at 06:00)
Condition: [Expiry Date] <= [Today + 90 days] AND [Renewal Alert Sent] = No
Action: Send email to [Reviewed By] and InfoSec team distribution list
Action: Set [Renewal Alert Sent] = Yes

Several vendors (Anthropic included, via their Trust Center) offer dedicated security portals where customers can download the latest SOC 2 report, view certifications, and be notified of changes. Subscribing to these portals reduces the manual effort of tracking report renewal, but does not replace your internal repository — the portal is the source; your repository is the archived copy with your internal review annotations.

SOC 2 Type II reports and penetration test summaries are almost always shared under NDA. The evidence repository must enforce access controls that reflect these NDA obligations — not everyone in the organization has the right to view these documents, and sharing them with unauthorized parties (including other vendors during their own assessments) violates the NDA.

create a dedicated security group (e.g., 'Vendor Due Diligence') for the repository. Membership is limited to InfoSec team members, legal counsel, and the DPO. Do not grant broad access to the engineering or procurement teams.

some vendors watermark SOC 2 reports with the recipient organization's name. Do not distribute these documents beyond the access control group — watermarks make the source of any leak traceable back to your organization.
maintain a record of the NDA executed with each vendor and the date it was signed. The NDA may have its own expiry date that is different from the SOC 2 report expiry.

your own auditors (SOX, ISO) may request to see a vendor's SOC 2 report as evidence of your vendor risk controls. Most vendor NDAs permit sharing with your auditors under confidentiality; confirm this before sharing and document the permission.

The most common failure in vendor evidence management is missing a renewal. A SOC 2 report that expired three months ago is not an audit finding for the vendor — it is an audit finding for your organization, because it means your vendor risk management program has a gap. Proactive renewal tracking is essential.
Contact vendor to request new artifact; confirm estimated issuance date
If new artifact not yet received, escalate to vendor account executive
If still not received, notify CISO; assess whether to issue conditional risk acceptance pending renewal
Final escalation; if artifact expires without renewal, risk register entry moves to 'evidence gap — escalated'
Vendor placed on 'evidence gap' watchlist; further procurement blocked until renewed

When your organization is audited (SOX, ISO 27001, SOC 2, GDPR), auditors will request evidence of vendor risk management. Having well-organized evidence is the difference between a two-hour audit evidence session and a two-week evidence chase. Prepare for the following standard requests:

## Common Audit Evidence Requests — Vendor Risk Management

Request 1: "Provide your vendor risk management policy."
Response: Link to InfoSec policy document (version-controlled); confirm last review date.

Request 2: "List your critical and high-risk vendors."
Response: Export vendor register filtered to Tier 1 and Tier 2; include last assessment date and risk tier justification.

Request 3: "Provide evidence of due diligence for [Vendor X]."
Response: Package from evidence repository:
  - SOC 2 report (with cover showing report date and observation period)
  - Risk register entry (with review date and approver)
  - DPA (signed copy)
  - Any exception findings and risk acceptance documentation

Request 4: "How do you track vendor certification renewals?"
Response: Screenshot of renewal calendar / Power Automate flow; evidence of most recent renewal alert emails.

Request 5: "What happens when a vendor fails to renew their SOC 2 on time?"
Response: Escalation procedure document; example of 'evidence gap' watchlist entry if applicable.

Audit tip: prepare a standard evidence package for Tier 1 vendors (all artifacts above)
and Tier 2 vendors (questionnaire + risk register entry) before the audit starts,
rather than scrambling during fieldwork.

Despite best efforts, evidence gaps occur: a vendor issues their SOC 2 late, a DPA is in redline longer than expected, or a newly onboarded vendor's questionnaire response has not yet been reviewed. Document evidence gaps explicitly rather than hoping they go unnoticed.

when an artifact is pending renewal, document a conditional risk acceptance: the vendor is approved to continue operating under the expired artifact for a defined grace period (typically 30 days), on condition that the renewal is received within that period. The CISO or delegate signs the conditional acceptance.

if a vendor cannot provide a standard artifact (e.g., a startup without a SOC 2 yet), document the compensating controls that substitute: a custom security questionnaire with detailed responses, a right-to-audit clause in the contract, a reference customer security review.

any vendor with an evidence gap older than 30 days should be escalated to the CISO. Any gap older than 60 days should trigger a formal review of whether to continue the relationship or block further procurement until evidence is received.

Evidence repository programs evolve over time as an organization's vendor portfolio grows and audit requirements mature. Organizations typically pass through three maturity levels, each with distinct capabilities and gaps.
Artifacts stored in email or personal drives; no expiry tracking; retrieved on demand during audits under pressure
Email attachments; personal Google Drive or OneDrive folders
Centralize into a shared location with consistent naming and access control
Shared repository with consistent folder structure and metadata; manual renewal reminders via calendar; access controlled
SharePoint library; Confluence with metadata; manual calendar alerts
Automate renewal alerts; link repository to contract lifecycle management
GRC platform with automated workflows; questionnaire distribution, evidence upload, and renewal alerts in one system; audit reports generated on demand
Continuous monitoring integration (auto-pull SOC 2 from vendor portals; auto-flag policy changes)

Most organizations underestimate the effort to move from Level 1 to Level 2. The technical step (setting up a SharePoint library) takes an afternoon. The organizational step (getting all existing vendor artifacts consolidated and tagged with expiry dates) takes weeks and requires buy-in from InfoSec, Legal, and Procurement.
Integrating the Repository with Contract Lifecycle Management

Due diligence evidence and contracts have different owners (InfoSec vs. Legal) and different systems, but they describe the same vendor relationships. The most common operational failure is that a contract renews automatically while the security team is unaware, leaving the vendor risk register stale or the DPA on outdated terms.

Every contract renewal in your CLM system (Ironclad, Conga, DocuSign CLM, or even a spreadsheet) should trigger a review gate for the evidence repository. The contract renewal workflow must not complete until InfoSec confirms that the vendor's SOC 2 is current and the DPA terms are acceptable for the renewal period.

## Contract Renewal Checklist — InfoSec Review Gate

Triggered by: Contract renewal date - 60 days
Required approver: InfoSec lead + DPO (if personal data in scope)

1. Security evidence:
   - [ ] SOC 2 Type II (or equivalent) is current and covers the renewal period
     If not: request updated report from vendor before approving renewal
   - [ ] Penetration test summary is within 12 months
   - [ ] Vendor questionnaire updated within 12 months (or confirm no material changes)

2. Privacy evidence:
   - [ ] DPA terms are current and cover all data categories in use
   - [ ] Sub-processor list reviewed; no unacceptable additions since last review
   - [ ] BAA current (if HIPAA in scope)

3. Risk register:
   - [ ] Vendor risk register entry reviewed and approved for the new contract period
   - [ ] Any previously identified risk exceptions or findings: confirm status

4. Contract terms:
   - [ ] Security exhibit (if present) terms are still appropriate
   - [ ] Incident notification SLA is contractually confirmed
   - [ ] Termination and data deletion clauses are present

Outcome:
  - All items checked: InfoSec approves renewal → CLM workflow proceeds
  - Open items: renewal placed on hold pending resolution; escalate to CISO if
    resolution not achieved within 10 business days

The evidence repository must retain historical artifacts, not just the current version. Audits frequently cover a historical period (e.g., the previous 12 months for SOC 2, or the period under dispute for a breach investigation). If you replaced last year's SOC 2 with this year's without retaining the prior version, you cannot demonstrate that your vendor risk controls were operating during the prior period.
retain all historical versions for a minimum of 7 years. The current version is the primary document; prior versions are archived.
retain all executed versions for the life of the contract plus 7 years. The DPA version in effect at the time of any incident is critical for litigation and regulatory response.
retain all versions; they show the vendor's security posture evolution over time and are useful context for gap analyses.
retain all historical states of the risk register entry, including approvers, dates, and rationale for risk acceptance decisions.

## Evidence File Naming Convention

Format: [VendorName]_[ArtifactType]_[YYYY]_[Version].pdf

Examples:
  Anthropic_SOC2TypeII_2025_v1.pdf        ← current year, first receipt
  Anthropic_SOC2TypeII_2025_v2.pdf        ← if vendor reissues (bridge letter added)
  Anthropic_SOC2TypeII_2024_v1.pdf        ← prior year (archived)
  Anthropic_DPA_Executed_2024-11-01.pdf   ← use execution date for contracts
  AcmeChatOps_SIGLite_2025-Q1.pdf        ← include quarter if updated mid-year
  AcmeChatOps_PenTestSummary_2025.pdf

Folder structure:
  /Vendor Evidence/
    /Anthropic/
      /SOC2/
      /DPA/
      /PenTest/
      /SubprocessorList/
    /AcmeChatOps/
      /SOC2/
      /DPA/
      /SIG/
    ...

Do NOT overwrite prior versions. Archive folder contains all superseded documents.

The evidence repository should feed into regular reporting to the CISO and, where relevant, to the board or audit committee. A monthly or quarterly vendor risk summary confirms that the program is operating effectively and surfaces any systemic issues (e.g., a vendor category that consistently misses renewal deadlines).
% of active Tier 1 vendors with all artifacts current (not expired)
% of artifact renewals received before expiry (no grace period required)
For any current evidence gap: how many days past expiry
0 days — any gap triggers immediate escalation
% of active vendors with a risk register entry and at least one security artifact
100% for Tier 1 and Tier 2; tracking only for Tier 3
Time to produce complete evidence package for a specific vendor under audit request

Tracking audit retrieval time is particularly valuable: it forces the team to actually practice retrieving evidence before an audit, which surfaces gaps in folder organization, access control issues (who has the password to the NDA repository?), and missing or incomplete artifacts. Running a quarterly evidence retrieval drill — picking a random Tier 1 vendor and timing how long it takes to assemble a complete evidence package — builds the operational muscle memory that prevents audit panic.

A due diligence evidence repository is the operational foundation of your vendor risk management program. It stores all assessment artifacts (SOC 2, DPA, pen test, SIG, sub-processor list, BAA), enforces NDA-compliant access controls, drives automated renewal alerts at 90/60/30/7-day thresholds, and provides audit-ready evidence packages for SOX, ISO, GDPR, and SOC 2 reviews. The repository is most effective when integrated into a GRC platform or structured SharePoint library with consistent metadata, and when renewal tracking is automated rather than relying on manual calendar reminders. Evidence gaps should be managed through conditional risk acceptance with explicit CISO sign-off, not silently deferred.
